chapters.title: Creating a healthy work environment
chapters.text:
How different would our lives be if there were no injustice? Perhaps you've wondered about this before or maybe dismissed such an idea because it seemed too good to be true.

While the author does not answer this philosophical question, she provides instruction and specific examples on making the world better by locating, analyzing, and fighting injustice.

Mainly relating to workplace ethics, the book dives into the unfairness behind various work processes in most organizations. To transform these processes, Kim Scott offers a new approach to managing organizations called Just Work: a healthy space where the employees can unlock their full potential and enhance their creativity and productivity.

No employee works well in a toxic environment, especially underrepresented people in non-inclusive workplaces. When you have to put up with bias, prejudice, or even bullying every day, there is very little energy left to put into achieving goals and meeting deadlines. After all, we all work better and are more motivated if we feel appreciated.

According to a McKinsey study, workplaces with inequality and mostly homogenous staff, like all-white teams, are less productive and come up with fewer creative solutions than heterogeneous teams with various cultures and genders represented.

If you want to change society, start with yourself.

“What can I do?” — you might ask. The answer is simple: you can change the world. No matter your position, social group, or skin color, you have the power to tip the scales in favor of the good. You may be wondering how you can do that, and the answers are all here; the book provides valuable insights for all sides of unjust situations. So, no matter what role you play in a particular unfair scenario, you will know how to react and what to do to minimize the possibility of it happening again.

Moreover, despite not being a novelty, the inequality and injustice question has become more profound in recent years, spurred by the BlackLivesMatter and MeToo movements. The events preceding these movements made the unfairness so visible that it's no longer possible to ignore it. And taking into account all the current economic, social, and environmental issues, the need to maintain just and equal work environments is greater than ever, as workers are more vulnerable than they've ever been.

chapters.title: Injustice in the workplace: roles and responsibilities
chapters.text:
The three leading causes of injustice in the workplace are bias, prejudice, and bullying. In short, bias denotes an unconscious impulse to jump to conclusions, prejudice implies doing the same but consciously, and bullying is acting on it and being outright mean. Each of these causes requires a specific response.

When fighting these problems, be aware of the different perspectives of the people involved to have enough insight to decide on the best course of action. In any unjust situation in your workplace, you will play one or more of the four possible roles:
• Victim
• Upstander
• One who caused harm
• Leader

The author provides some advice for each role specifically:

If you are a victim, choose your battles and think about whether to speak up or keep silent. There is nothing wrong with not saying anything when you realize that it will be too costly for you and your career.

If you notice a display of injustice, be an upstander, and do not just observe; take action. If you feel that something is wrong, the best reaction is to stand up and report it. If that is not an option, the least you can do is comfort the person harmed.

If you are the one who caused harm, pay attention to what people accuse you of, consider what you did wrong, and if you didn't do it on purpose, prevent it from happening next time.

If you are a leader, do not just punish the bad behavior; make sure to encourage your team to collaborate. Teach subordinates to be mindful of any bias or prejudice that might affect their relationships with co-workers.

It is better to fight injustice and make mistakes than to do nothing at all.

Forgive yourself if you fail to act appropriately on the spot. Striving for justice takes a lot of courage and determination; you need constant practice to know how to react instantly. Embrace any failure as an opportunity to learn from your mistakes. Set an example: once people notice changes in your behavior, explain the difference behind them and encourage others to follow your lead.

chapters.title: Bias and how to deal with it
chapters.text:
One reason it was so hard to act quickly in an uncomfortable workplace situation was that you were unsure of the other person's intention. It may have been an honest slip-up or an intentional insult.

Here are some of the common bias-based incidents in the workplace:
• Belittling words: calling women “girls” or addressing men as “ladies” as a form of insult
• Stereotype-based expectations: expecting women to be timid and silent, and men to be strong and rough
• Confusing a person's role: assuming a Latino person is a service staff based on their ethnicity

When receiving offending actions or words, use “I” statements to invite the person to view the situation from your perspective. Imagine a situation where you are the only woman in an all-male team, and your co-workers with the same positional status constantly expect you to take notes whenever there is a meeting. You can say the following: “I don't think I can actively participate in the meeting when I need to take notes all the time. Can someone else take notes today?”

There are many benefits in addressing bias instead of ignoring it. When you assert yourself, you show it is beneficial and encourage others to speak up. By voicing your observation about bias, you help the other person know how others in the office perceive their actions, so they are aware of the areas that need improvement. Plus, you make it easier for them to get along with you by offering sincere and polite feedback.

People who are committed to being good people want to know about the bad things they do so they can make amends and avoid doing them again. ~ Kim Scott

Biases are not your fault; you are unaware of them because they were imposed on you by the community where you grew up. However, it is wrong to overlook the fact that you've made a biased assumption when someone points it out for you.

When you notice your bias, correct it. Apologize to the person and make sure to mention what exactly you did that was wrong. It might be hard, but it's worth it: the person would appreciate someone else acknowledging the problem they face all the time.

Fighting bias is a continuous process — you are not done by only hearing feedback.

chapters.title: Dealing with prejudice and bullying
chapters.text:
Prejudice is harder to confront than bias because it is a conscious belief. You will not succeed just by pointing it out since the person is aware of what they mean by saying this and that. Even though it's probably pointless to try and make that person change their opinion, the main reason to say anything at all is to underline that, regardless of what they think, they have no right to impose their views on you. Use the “It” statement to confront prejudice, for example: “It is degrading/offensive of you to say this to me because...”

Sometimes it makes more sense to agree to disagree rather than enter a full-blown conflict.

After the “It” statement, you might want to communicate with the person further and try to make them reconsider. These might be your motivations for doing so:
• To show the person your perspective and to not be silenced
• To initiate a discussion to strengthen your argument
• To find something you both agree upon that might be a precedent for a friendship later on

There is no perfect script to use for situations when being bullied. But you can write out a couple of “You” statements that feel right and match your language. It can be something like “You’re being inappropriate, rude, etc.” After that, practice saying them out loud in front of the mirror or to the other person.

Most people will respect boundaries—if they know where they are. ~ Kim Scott

As a leader, you will have to confront prejudice and bullying at some point. It is not your place to tell people what to believe, but it is your job to tell them what they can or cannot do in the workplace, so there are two things you should do immediately. First, establish a code of conduct so that all employees know their rights and communicate with co-workers properly and without being disrespectful. Second, underline clear consequences to bullying behavior up to termination, which should discourage people from doing so.

Did you know? According to a 2017 study conducted by Workplace Bullying Institute, 19% of Americans are bullied in the workplace while 19% more have witnessed it, making 60 million people affected by bullying overall.

chapters.title: Fighting with harassment and discrimination
chapters.text:
Discrimination happens when power mixes with bias and prejudice, and harassment is power with bias or bullying. While fighting bias in a workplace is important, fighting harassment is even more so.

Power tends to corrupt, and absolute power corrupts absolutely. ~ Lord Acton

When power is unchecked, harassment issues become much more likely. It is important to delegate authority to multiple people on the team to avoid creating a hostile, bully-welcoming environment and disempowering employees.

Creating hiring committees is one of the ways to go: they make better hiring decisions than individuals because it is easier to spot someone else's bias than your own. Having these committees lowers the chances of sexual harassment during the hiring process. However, they can only be fully unbiased and fair if they consist of different race and gender representatives, offering different perspectives.

What if the CEO is the one whose behavior is a problem? The system must ensure that the accountability process can go around the CEO if needed and up to the board of directors. Or the leader can hire a specific person who will oversee all complaints and be an objective third party, whom the employees will feel safe to approach.

It is understandable when people harmed are reluctant to say something and go public: it is risky for their career. However, most of us overestimate the risks involved. There are also such benefits at stake like restoring justice, or at least vocalizing it so that people are aware of it; it saves our self-respect and the feeling of self-agency, helping other possible or real victims.

Despite a common misconception, vulnerability is a strength, not a weakness. Exposing yourself to the world knowing you can't control the outcome shows undeniable courage.

If you see that someone is suffering from discrimination in the workplace, your job as an upstander is to help them find a safe exit. For example, you save your colleague from being gaslighted when you admit that you have noticed how badly someone has treated them. And by introducing them to someone who can offer practical advice, like a lawyer or a professional coach, you allow them to negotiate from a place of strength instead of fear.

chapters.title: Systems of injustice
chapters.text:
To fight systemic injustice, we must acknowledge that it is a whole system that is unfair and needs to change. If we refuse to admit to having problems as a society, we make ourselves unable to fix them. We can implement the Just Work system if we strive for collaboration and respect for individuality. The opposite is striving for coercion and conformity — and that is what we need to steer clear of.

Conformity excludes the underrepresented people and is a hotbed for bias, prejudice, and bullying. It is an attitude that is not as violent as coercion, but it is no less sexist and racist. It operates in the shadows and hides behind day-to-day behavior in little actions or phrases, making it dangerous. You can observe conformity in action when, for example, a white manager only hires white employees or when males on a team constantly talk over a female teammate or only perceive her as a decorative element rather than a valuable worker.

Coercion dynamics lead from bias to violence. Often the unconscious bias can quickly and dramatically lead to outright violence, like when George Floyd was murdered by a police officer minutes after being suspected of forging a $20 check.

Where there is injustice towards one group of people, there will be injustice towards all.

In our fight against unfairness, we shouldn't forget that it is equally important not to contradict our beliefs with our actions. Being unfair to unfair people only contributes to the problem we are trying to fix, it’s not a solution. Plus, by behaving that way, we do not change a person's beliefs — we just make them defensive and even more convinced about the accuracy of their opinion. Remember that hate corrupts from the inside and only leads to more hatred.

In short, on your way to Just Work, you will have to face many issues. If sometimes it all seems too much, remember the two main principles to adhere to:
• Accept your colleagues for who they are, don't try to impose your view on how they should behave.
• Strive to collaborate with people instead of aiming to dominate them.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Just Work is a healthy approach to professional ethics designed to create optimal conditions for employees to collaborate, grow, and be productive. A just and fair environment is vital to workers' motivation and, as a result, to a company's success. It shows how to communicate with other people in a respectful, healthy way even if the people you are dealing with are very different from you or have opposing thoughts and beliefs. It also saves you from pointless arguments and feelings of irritation and frustration that usually come next.

Unfortunately, most modern workplaces have a lot of issues concerning racism, sexism, and other manifestations of inequality, and we as a society need to join our efforts in fighting them to the best of our ability. Imagine how much faster humanity's progress would go if we were collaborating instead of competing, and how much more pleasant life would be if we embraced each other's individuality instead of overgeneralizing everyone!

The book explains how you can deal with bias, prejudice, bullying, discrimination, and harassment in the workplace, but who says this knowledge is only applicable to work? These issues reflect every corner of our lives, so we can put this knowledge to our everyday use.

The next time someone tells you a sexist joke, suspects that a woman they know is a lesbian because of her short haircut, or casually remarks that Asian people are bad drivers — react. Express your dissatisfaction gently but point out how such words are perceived. Who knows, perhaps your honest feedback would be the reason someone notices their biased behavior and decides to fix it.

No matter your social status, positional power, sexuality, gender, or race, you can contribute to a collective fight against inequality. In different scenarios, you could be the victim, the aggressor, the leader, or the upstander. The point is, you can do good while playing any of these roles if you adhere to the principles of Just Work. And it is incredibly insightful to experience these roles because that is how your perspective widens and shows you a bigger picture.

Try this

Start with yourself: ask your friend or colleague to listen to you daily to notice any bias you might express. By monitoring your unconscious misconceptions, you can begin the process of learning and improvement. Only by acknowledging your issues can you start fixing them, and when you do — you will be able to set an example of fighting injustice and teach others how to do so.

Read it till the end?
